Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Arrowleaf balsamroot (Balsamorhiza sagittata)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This plant has large arrow-shaped leaves and is locally abundant in the west and northwest Black Hills. The large yellow flowers can put on quite a show on higher elevation limestone slopes.

images.title: Arrowleaf balsamroot
images.description: Arrowleaf balsamroot (Balsamorhiza sagittata) in bloom on Bearlodge Ranger District. Rattlesnake Canyon, WY by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Darkthroat shootingstar (Dodecatheon pulchellum)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This plant has clusters downward-pointing, purple flowers have a sweet clove scent. It is common in open pine forest, meadows and clearings.

images.title: Darkthroat shootingstar (Dodecatheon pulchellum)
images.description: Darkthroat shootingstar (Dodecatheon pulchellum) in bloom along Gillette Canyon Rd in a Black Hills Montane Grassland by Kelly Warnke, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Desert biscuitroot (Lomatium foeniculaceum)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing forb has clusters of yellow flowers and dissected leaves resembling those of a carrot. It grows on dry foothills and plains.

images.title: Desert Biscuitroot
images.description: Desert Biscuitroot (Lomatium foeniculaceum) taken on the Hell Canyon Trail by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest.

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Downy paintbrush (Castilleja sessiliflora)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This soft, hairy plant has linear leaves that are arranged spirally around the stem. Flowers occur in a loose spike and are pale yellow with tinges of pink. It grows from low to mid-elevations in mixed grass prairie.

images.title: Downy Paintedcup
images.description: (Castilleja sessiliflora) blooming south of Hot Springs, SD on the Hell Canyon Ranger District by C. Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Early cinquefoil (Potentilla concinna)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing plant has hairy compound leaves and yellow flowers. It is fairly common from mid to high elevations in mixed grass prairie, open forests and woodlands throughout the Black Hills.

images.title: Early cinquefoil
images.description: (Potentilla concinna) in bloom along Gillette Canyon Rd in a Black Hills Montane Grassland by Kelly Warnke, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Fairybells (Prosartes trachycarpa)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This moist forest plant has drooping greenish-white, bell-shaped flowers and produces flat-topped, bright orange berries in mid-summer. It grows in moist forest habitats from central to northern Black Hills. Photos in folder

images.title: Fairybells
images.description: (Forest Service photo by Cheryl Mayer)

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Goldenpea (Thermopsis rhombifolia)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: Goldenpea usually grows in wide-spread patches of many plants. It has three-parted leaves and showy yellow flowers and is common from low to high elevations in a variety of habitats throughout the Black Hills. Photo in folder

images.title: Goldenpea
images.description: (Forest Service photo by Cheryl Mayer)

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Heartleaf arnica (Arnica cordifolia)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: As the name suggests, this one has heart-shaped leaves that are soft to the touch. It grows from mid to high elevations on limestone in the northern Black Hills.

images.title: Heartleaf arnica
images.description: (Arnica cordifolia) in bloom in the Bear Lodge Mountains by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Kinnikinnik (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: Also known as bearberry, this mat-forming evergreen plant produces white to pink urn-shaped flowers and pea-sized red berries. It is a common understory species in open pine forest.

images.title: Kinnikinnick
images.description: (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi) in berry near Bear Butte Creek on the Northern Hills Ranger District by Cheryl Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Lanceleaf bluebells (Mertensia lanceolata)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This plant has bluish-green leaves and produces clusters of drooping blue bell-shaped flowers in early spring. It grows in grasslands, open forests and rocky slopes from low to high elevations.

images.title: Prairie Bluebells
images.description: (Mertensia lanceolata) in bloom in the Witch Springs area of the Hell Canyon Ranger District by C. Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Longspur violet (Viola adunca)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This common violet has leafy stems, purple flowers and grows in forests, woodlands and meadows throughout the Black Hills.

images.title: Longspur violet
images.description: (Viola adunca) in flower along Hell Canyon Trail by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Meadow deathcamas (Zigadenus venenosus)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: As the name suggests, this one is poisonous! It grows in dry mixed grass prairie and open pine forest.

images.title: Meadow deathcamas
images.description: (Zigadenus veneosus) blooming in the Bear Lodge Mountains, Bearlodge Ranger District by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Oregon grape (Mahonia repens)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing, evergreen shrub has holly-like leaves with pointy margins. The sweet-smelling yellow flowers are loosely clustered. It grows on moist rocky forest slopes throughout the Black Hills.

images.title: Oregon Grape
images.description: (Mahonia repens) in the riparian area along Spearfish Creek in Spearfish Canyon by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Pasque flower (Pulsatilla patens)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: The lovely pale purple, yellow-centered pasque flower is one of the first flowers to bloom in early spring, and it is the state flower of South Dakota. The whole plant is softly fuzzy and in mid-summer it produces wispy tufted fruit clusters. It is common in mixed grass prairie and open pine forest from low to high elevations.

images.title: Pasqueflower
images.description: (Pulsatilla patens or Anemone patens) about to bloom in Boulder Canyon by Cheryl Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Prairie chickweed (Cerastium arvense)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: Also known as mouse-eared chickweed, this plant grows in loose mats and has white flowers with deeply notched petals. It is common throughout the Black Hills in a variety of habitats.

images.title: Prairie chickweed
images.description: (Cerastium arvense) in bloom along the Mickelson Trail, Hell Canyon District by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Richardson’s geranium (Geranium richardsonii)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This plant has lobed leaves and white flowers with 5 petals. It grows along streams and moist forests in the central and northern Black Hills.

images.title: Richardson's geranium
images.description: (Geranium richardsonii) in flower by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Rocky Mountain iris (Iris missouriensis)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: Large showy light-purple flowers and flattened linear leaves make the Rocky Mountain iris unmistakable. It grows in moist meadows, clearings and open forests across the Black Hills.

images.title: Rocky Mountain Iris
images.description: (Iris missouriensis) blooming along the Hell Canyon Trail, Hell Canyon Ranger District by Chelsea Monks USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Star lily (Leucocrinum montanum)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing plant has tufted grass-like leaves and showy white flowers that appear at ground level. It grows from low to high elevations in prairie to open pine habitats.

images.title: Starlily
images.description: (Leucocrinum montanum) on Hell Canyon Trail by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Starry false Solomon’s seal (Maianthemum stellatum)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This plant has smooth, alternate parallel-veined leaves and produces a few staggered white flowers at the tip of its stem. It grows in open forests and woodlands throughout the Black Hills.

images.title: Starry false lily of the valley
images.description: (Maianthemum stellatum) blooming in the Bear Lodge Mountains, Bearlodge Ranger District by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Sulphur paintbrush (Castilleja sulphurea)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: The greenish-yellow flowers of this plant occur in tufty spikes at the top of upright stems. It is common in coniferous and mixed forests from central to northern Black Hills.

images.title: Sulphur Indian paintbrush
images.description: Sulphur Indian paintbrush (Castilljea sulphurea) in bloom along the Hell Canyon Trail. Pale greenish-white bracts at ends of lanceolate-leaved stems. Green seed heads exerted by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Threadleaf musineon (Musineon tenuifolium)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing forb has finely dissected leaves and clusters of yellow flowers. It is found throughout the Black Hills and grows in open forests and grasslands with dry, rocky soil.

images.title: Slender wildparsley
images.description: Musineon tenuifolium in bloom along Hell Canyon Trail by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Tufted milkvetch (Astragalus spatulatus)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: This plant forms low-growing, dense tufts, has silvery vegetation and produces clusters of vibrant bright purple flowers. It grows on dry hilltops, ridges and rocky slopes in grasslands and open pine forest.

images.title: Tufted milkvetch
images.description: (Astragalus spatulatus) in bloom by Cheryl Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Spring to early-summer wildflowers.name: Wild strawberry (Fragaria virginiana)
Spring to early-summer wildflowers.description: You are probably familiar with this plant’s three-parted leaves, white flowers and red fleshy fruit! The fruit is just as tasty, but much smaller than the strawberries you find in the store. It is common throughout the Black Hills and grows in moist meadows and open pine and mixed forests.

images.title: Wild strawberry
images.description: (Fragaria virginiana) blooming along the Hell Canyon Trail, Hell Canyon Ranger District by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Blackeyed Susan (Rudbeckia hirta)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This plant is easy to recognize in flower, with long yellow ray petals and a dark center. It is common throughout the Black Hills in moist meadows, open forests and roadsides.

images.title: Blackeyed Susan
images.description: Rudbeckia hirta is commonly found in wildflower seed mixes. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Blanket flower (Gaillardia aristata)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: The showy flower heads of this plant have yellow ray petals and reddish-purple tinged centers. It grows in grasslands, roadsides and open areas.

images.title: Blanket flower
images.description: Gaillardia aristata in bloom in Dugout Gulch Botanical Area. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Blue-eyed grass (Sisyrinchium montanum)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: Blue-eyed grass is not actually a grass, but a member of the Iris family. It grow in meadows, grasslands and open forests throughout the Black Hills.

images.title: Strict blue-eyed grass
images.description: Purple flower with acuminate petals and a yellow center. Leaves are grass-like. Also known as Sisyrinchium montanum. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Bunchberry (Cornus canadensis)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing plant appears to have a single white flower, but actually has a cluster of many flowers with 4 petal-like bracts. In late summer it produces a tight cluster of bright red fruits. It grows in rich moist aspen, birch or mixed coniferous forest at mid to high elevations.

images.title: Bunchberry
images.description: (Forest Service photo by Cheryl Mayer)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Common yarrow (Achillea millefolium)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This common plant has flat-topped clusters of white flower heads and grows throughout the Black Hills in a variety of habitats.

images.title: Common yarrow
images.description: Achillea millefolium in bloom in Dugout Gulch Botanical Area. Bearlodge Ranger District. Black Hills National Forest. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Cow parsnip (Heracleum maximum)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This tall thick-stemmed plant has large lobed leaves and large flat-topped clusters of white flowers. It grows in moist, rich woods, usually along streams.

images.title: Cow Parsnip
images.description: (Forest Service photo by Cheryl Mayer)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Field mint (Mentha arvensis)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This is a common plant along stream banks and other wet places. Its pale purple flowers are found in clusters along the square stem. Photo in folder

images.title: Field mint
images.description: (Forest Service photo by Cheryl Mayer)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Harebell (Campanula rotundifolia)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: Harebell’s light purple, drooping bell-shaped flowers are produced on upright stems and can be found from mid-summer to early fall. It is common from low to high elevations in a variety of habitats.

images.title: Harebell
images.description: Campanula rotundifolia in bloom in Dugout Gulch Botanical Area. Bearlodge Ranger District. Black Hills National Forest. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Heartleaf alexanders (Zizia aptera)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: Heartleaf alexanders has thick smooth leaves and flat-topped clusters of bright yellow flowers. It grows in moist forests and meadows at mid to high elevations of central to northern Black Hills.

images.title: Heartleaf alexanders
images.description: Zizia aptera flower. Along the South Fork of Castle Creek, Mystic District. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Prairie smoke (Geum triflorum)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing plant forms mat-like clumps. It has drooping purple-tinged flowers, but is most noticeable in fruit when it shows off its feathery smoke-like appearance. It grows from low to high elevations in mixed grass prairie and montane grassland habitat.

images.title: Prairie smoke
images.description: Geum triflorum (also known as old man's whiskers) in bloom along Gillette Canyon Rd in a Black Hills Montane Grassland. (Photo by Kelly Warnke, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Purple coneflower (Echinacea angustifolia)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This taprooted perennial produces distinct flower heads on tall coarsely hairy stems. Flowers have long pink ray petals and yellowish-brown prickly centers. It grows from low to mid elevations in mixed grass prairie and open pine forests. It is known for its medicinal use as an anesthetic and an immune system stimulant.

images.title: Purple coneflower
images.description: Echinacea angustifolia in bloom in Dugout Gulch Botanical Area. Bearlodge Ranger District. Black Hills National Forest. (Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest)

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Spearleaf stonecrop (Sedum lanceolatum)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This small succulent plant has showy yellow star-shaped flowers and grows on rocky thin soils in open pine forest.

images.title: Spearleaf Stonecrop (Sedum lanceolatum)
images.description: Spearleaf Stonecrop (Sedum lanceolatum) has succulent leaves and yellow flowers. Photo by Cheryl Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest.

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Spreading dogbane (Apocynum androsaemifolium)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This plant has reddish colored stems, milky sap and numerous pink bell-shaped flowers. It is common at low to mid-elevations on forest edges and openings.

images.title: Spreading Dogbane (Apocynum androsaemifolium)
images.description: Spreading dogbane (Apocynum androsaemifolium) has pink bell-shaped flowers. Photo by Cheryl Mayer, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest.

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Stemless four-nerve daisy (Tetraneuris acaulis)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This low-growing plant has linear leaves that grow in tufts. Its yellow flower heads occur on upright stems and bloom for much of the summer. It grows in various dry habitats from low to high elevations.

images.title: Stemless Four-Nerve Daisy (Tetraneuris acaulis)
images.description: Tetraneuris acaulis in bloom near Witch Spring, Hell Canyon Ranger District. Photo by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest.

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Twinflower (Linnaea borealis)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: This low, trailing plant is slightly woody and produces a pair of pink, nodding, bell-shaped flowers. It grows in moist pine and spruce forests from central to northern Black Hills.

images.title: Twinflower (Linnaea borealis)
images.description: Linnaea borealis in bloom by Chelsea Monks, USDA Forest Service/Black Hills National Forest.

Mid-summer wildflowers.name: Wood lily (Lilium philadelphicum)
Mid-summer wildflowers.description: Wood lily’s large orange flowers are produced on upright stems with whorls of leaves. It grows in open pine forests, deciduous woodlands and meadows from central to northern Black Hills.

images.title: Wood Lily (Lilium philadelphicum)
images.description: Wood Lily has a large showy orange flower. Photo by Cheryl Mayer, Black Hills National Forest/USDA Forest Service

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Broom snakeweed (Gutierrezia sarothrae)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This low compact shrub grows up to a foot in height, has fine branches, narrow leaves and produces small flat-topped clusters of yellow flowers in late summer. It grows on dry barren sites and open woodlands at low elevations.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Curlycup gumweed (Grindelia squarrosa)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This sticky, yellow-flowered plant is common in dry pastures, roadsides and disturbed areas.

images.title: Curlycup gumweed
images.description: Grindelia squarrosa in bloom near Sundance, WY. Photo by Chelsea Monks, Black Hills National Forest.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Dotted gayfeather (Liatris punctata)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This plant has narrow leaves crowed along the stem and lovely pinkish-purple flowers arranged in an elongate spike. It grows from low to mid-elevations in mixed grass prairie and open pine forest habitat.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Goldenrods (Solidago)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: Goldenrods are upright yellow-flowered perennials that characterize the late summer landscape. There are several goldenrod species that grow in a variety of habitats across the Black Hills.

images.title: Goldenrod
images.description: Solidago sp. in bloom in Spearfish Canyon. Northern Hills Ranger District, near Roughlock Falls. Photo by Chelsea Monks, Black Hills National Forest.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Hairy goldenaster (Heterotheca villosa)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This low-growing woody plant is common in dry grasslands, meadows and open forest habitats.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Rubber rabbitbrush (Ericameria nauseosa)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This branched, mound-shaped shrub has gray-green stems linear leaves. In late summer to early fall it produces abundant flat-topped clusters of yellow flowers. It grows in dry grassland and foothills of the western Black Hills.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Smooth blue aster (Symphyotrichum laeve) (also see photo)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: Smooth blue aster produces several light-purple flower heads and has bluish-green leaves that clasp the stem. It is common in open forests and meadows.

images.title: Smooth Blue Aster
images.description: (Photo by Cheryl Mayer, Black Hills National Forest/USDA Forest Service)

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Spotted joe-pyeweed (Eutrochium maculatum)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This late summer bloomer has showy clusters of pinkish-purple flower and grows in wetlands and near streams and springs.

images.title: Spotted Joe-Pyeweed
images.description: Eupatoriadelphus maculatus var. bruneri. Taken near Iron Creek in the southern Black Hills, Black Elk Wilderness by Cheryl Mayer, Black Hills National Forest.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Threenerve fleabane (Erigeron subtrinervis)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: This plant has pretty purple flower heads with yellow centers.  It grows in meadows, open pine and mixed forest from low to mid-elevations.

Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.name: Wild bergamot (Monarda fistulosa)
Late-summer to early-fall wildflowers.description: Also known as horsemint, beebalm or wild oregano, this plant has tall upright stems and grows in dense colonies. It produces round-topped clusters of purple flowers and is common from low to high elevations in a variety of habitats. Its vegetation contains a natural antiseptic compound.

images.title: Wild Bergamont
images.description: Monarda fistulosa is also known as beebalm and horsemint. Taken near Schoolhouse Gulch in the northern Black Hills by Chelsea Monks, Black Hills National Forest.
